pages.title: Welcome to Student Device and Account Safety
pages.subtitle: Learn how to protect school devices, student accounts, and classroom technology from misuse or unauthorized access.

video.caption: Introduction to Student Device and Account Safety

pages.title: Why device and account safety matters
pages.text:
School devices and student accounts often provide access to classwork, email, cloud storage, grades, and other school systems.

If a device or account is left unsecured, another person may be able to view information, submit work, change settings, or access school resources without permission.

This is also a cybersecurity issue because unauthorized access, unsafe browsing, weak passwords, and careless device use can expose school data or lead to misuse of school systems.

Simple habits like locking a device and protecting your password help prevent bigger cybersecurity problems.

image.alt: Student using a school device safely - Made with AI (not real kids)

pages.title: School accounts should stay private
pages.text:
Each student should use their own school account and keep login details private.

  • Do not share passwords with classmates or friends.
  • Do not let someone borrow your account, even for a quick task.
  • Use a strong password if your school allows you to create one.
  • Keep sign-in details in a safe place if you need help remembering them.

Sharing accounts can expose personal information, schoolwork, and activity history.

Your school account should only be used by you.

pages.title: Shared devices require extra care
pages.text:
Many schools use shared devices such as Chromebooks, tablets, library computers, or lab devices.

When using a shared device, students should:

  • Log in only to their own account
  • Log out when finished
  • Close school applications when done
  • Check that the next user cannot access their files or account

Shared devices are helpful, but they increase the risk of accidental access if users forget to log out.

On a shared device, logging out is one of the most important safety steps.

pages.title: Why locking your device matters
pages.text:
A device should never be left unlocked and unattended.

An unlocked device may allow someone else to:

  • Read messages or email
  • Open school files
  • Change settings
  • Submit work or send messages as you
  • Access school systems without permission

Even a short moment away from a device can create risk. Locking your screen helps prevent unauthorized access and protects both your account and school information.

If you step away, lock the device.

pages.title: Be careful with downloads and websites
pages.text:
Students may sometimes click links or download files without realizing the risk.

Unsafe downloads, browser extensions, and suspicious websites can cause problems on school devices.

  • Use approved websites and school-assigned tools
  • Avoid unexpected pop-ups, fake alerts, or strange download prompts
  • Do not install software or extensions unless allowed
  • Ask a teacher or IT support if something looks unusual

Clicking the wrong link can affect more than one device or account.

image.alt: Safe browsing and download practices on school devices

pages.title: Check privacy and security settings
pages.text:
School apps, accounts, and devices may have privacy and security settings that help protect information.

  • Review account settings if your school allows it
  • Use school-approved privacy options when available
  • Be careful what information an app can access
  • Ask a teacher or IT support if you are unsure whether a setting should be changed

Small settings can make a difference in how information is shared or protected.

Reference: U.S. Department of Education K-12 Cybersecurity

Check privacy and security settings before assuming an app or device is safe by default.

pages.title: Do not let others use your signed-in device
pages.text:
Even a short moment of access can create risk if someone else uses a device while you are still signed in.

They may be able to:

  • Send messages from your account
  • Open files or view personal information
  • Submit assignments or change work
  • Access school systems as if they were you

If another person needs access, they should use their own account or go through the proper school process.

Never assume a signed-in device is safe to share.

pages.title: Why this is also a cybersecurity issue
pages.text:
Device and account safety is not just about following rules. It is also part of cybersecurity.

  • Weak account habits can let someone else sign in as you
  • Unlocked devices can expose files, messages, and school systems
  • Unsafe clicks or downloads can put accounts and devices at risk
  • Careless sharing can expose student or school information to the wrong person

Cybersecurity includes protecting accounts, controlling access, and reducing the chance of unauthorized use.

Reference: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
Reference: U.S. Department of Education - Unauthorized Disclosure

Protecting your account and device helps protect both school systems and student information.

pages.title: What to do if something seems wrong
pages.text:
If a student account or device may be compromised:

  1. Stop using the device if needed
  2. Log out or lock the account if possible
  3. Tell an administrator, or IT support right away
  4. Change the password if instructed

Quick reporting can help prevent bigger problems and protect other users.

Report device or account problems quickly so the school can respond.
